title: Gamebook Format (GBF) v1.10.0
description: A universal data interchange format for representing interactive gamebooks as structured data. Designed to be consumed by a deterministic emulator that can play any gamebook without AI involvement at runtime.

metadata.description: Bibliographic information and parsing confidence report.

series.description: Series name (e.g., 'Fighting Fantasy', 'Lone Wolf'), or null if standalone.

series_number.description: Position within the series, or null.

year.description: Year of publication for this edition.

series_profile.description: Which rules profile this book follows.

total_sections.description: Expected total number of sections in the book.

codex_version.description: Version of the Gamebook Codex used to generate this file, if applicable.

parser_notes.description: Free-text notes about this specific parse (source quality, known issues, edition-specific details).

source_quality.description: Quality assessment of the source material.

computed_navigation.description: Sections requiring numeric or text input for navigation.

flagged_for_review.description: List of issues found during parsing. Either a free-form string, or an object with at least {section, issue} (or {section, note}) for richer attribution.

death_endings.description: Section ids that are death endings. Integer or string.

victory_endings.description: Section ids that are victory endings. Integer or string.

total_enemies.description: Total unique enemies in the enemies_catalog.

total_items.description: Total unique items in the items_catalog.

frontmatter.description: Introductory and supplementary material presented before the numbered sections AND/OR accessible to the player as reference material during play. Includes story background, rules explanations, maps, character sheets, equipment lists, glossaries, errata, and appendices. Pages are ordered for first-time presentation but may be marked individually as accessible-during-play so the emulator can expose them via an in-game reference panel at any time.

pages.description: Ordered list of frontmatter pages. By default these are shown before character creation AND accessible from the in-game reference panel during play. Pages may opt out of either behavior individually using the show_at_start and accessible_during_play fields.

title.description: Page title (e.g., 'The Story So Far', 'Rules', 'Map of Sommerlund', 'Kai Disciplines', 'Errata').

text.description: Full text content of this page. Markdown-style paragraph breaks are preserved.

type.description: Category of frontmatter content. The emulator may use this to group pages in the in-game reference panel (e.g., all 'map' pages together, all 'rules' pages together).

show_at_start.description: If true (default), this page is included in the pre-character-creation walkthrough that the emulator shows when the user starts a new game. If false, the page is only accessible from the in-game reference panel and is skipped at game start. Use false for reference pages that aren't part of the introductory narrative (e.g., an errata appendix that's only useful for lookup, or a map that's distracting at game start).

accessible_during_play.description: If true (default), this page is available from the in-game reference panel at any time during play, so the player can consult maps, rules summaries, glossaries, and the like without losing their place. If false, the page is shown only at game start and not accessible later. Use false for one-time intro material like 'The Story So Far' that doesn't need to be re-read mid-adventure.

image.description: Optional image reference for the page (e.g., 'illustration_map_sommerlund'). Used by emulators that can render maps or character sheet illustrations. The image identifier should match an entry in the book's illustrations index if one exists.

rules.description: The complete game system as parsed from the book's rules section. Every field should reflect what the specific book states, not series-level assumptions.

stats.description: All character stats tracked during the game.

attack_stat.description: The stat name used for player attack/combat skill, if the book's combat system uses one (e.g., whatever stat appears in `rules.stats[].name` that the round_script adds to compute the player's attack strength or combat skill). Must match a name in the stats array when set. May be null for threshold-based combat systems that use fixed hit thresholds and damage bonuses rather than a player-attack-vs-enemy-attack comparison (for example, books where each enemy has a `hit_threshold` the player must roll at-or-above on 2d6 plus a fixed modifier, with no per-combatant attack stat). When null, the round_script must read whatever per-combatant fields it needs directly from `player` and `enemy` without relying on a pre-computed attack stat.

health_stat.description: The stat name used for player health/hit points. When this reaches 0, the character is dead. Must match a name in the stats array. Unlike `attack_stat`, this is required because the emulator's death check depends on it.

combat_system.description: Combat system identifier or detailed rules object. May be a string shorthand (e.g., 'fighting_fantasy_standard') or a full object.

combat_rules_detail.description: Detailed combat parameters. Used when combat_system is a string shorthand.

currency_display_name.description: Display name for the game's currency used in the stat bar, inventory panel, and any 'you have X currency' UI strings. Defaults to 'Gold' if omitted. Use whatever term the book's rules section uses for its currency — examples across gamebooks include 'Gold Pieces', 'Gold Crowns', 'Silver Pennies', 'Doubloons', 'Credits', 'Bits', etc. The internal stat name used in event and condition references is still `gold` regardless of display name; this field only affects what the UI renders.

inventory_capacity.description: Shorthand for max inventory size. Null = unlimited.

multiple_enemies.description: How multi-enemy encounters are handled. e.g., 'as_instructed_per_section'.

description.description: Full book text describing this ability. Do not summarize.

requires_roll.description: If this ability requires a random roll when selected. For example, a weapon-specialization discipline might require a die roll to determine which weapon the character is specialized in, or a spell-selection ability might require a roll on a spell table.

prompt.description: Text shown to the player when rolling.

results.description: Mapping of roll values to result labels.

special_mechanics.description: Any book-specific rules not covered by other fields.

character_creation.description: Ordered steps to create a new character.

sections.description: All game sections, keyed by section id. Usually a section number in string form (e.g. '1', '212', '400'), but synthetic sub-sections introduced by multi-section patterns (e.g. codex 7.6.8 subroutine sections with return-to-caller) may use descriptive string IDs like '161_goblin' or '161_return'. IDs must match /^[A-Za-z0-9_]+$/.

items_catalog.description: All items that can be found, purchased, or received in the game.

name.description: Stat name as it appears in the book (e.g., 'skill', 'stamina', 'luck', 'combat_skill', 'endurance').

generation.description: Dice formula for initial value (e.g., '1d6+6', '2d6+12', 'R10+10', 'fixed:20'). Optional as of schema v1.10+ / codex Rule 26 — stats initialised by a `distribute_points` character-creation step (or by another explicit initialiser like `set_resource`) have no dice formula and should omit this field. When absent, the `character_creation.steps[]` entries are authoritative for the starting value; the emulators' post-creation validation pass flags any declared stat still undefined after character creation completes.

initial_is_max.description: If true, stat cannot exceed its rolled starting value unless specifically instructed.

combat_system_detail.description: Detailed combat system parameters. The round_script field contains a Lua script that the emulator executes each combat round.

type.description: Combat system identifier. The emulator does not interpret this — it executes the round_script.

round_script.description: Lua script executed each combat round. The emulator provides these globals: player (table with .attack, .health, .name), enemy (same), combat (table with .round, .standard_damage, .last_result, .last_damage), roll(formula) function, log(msg) function, lookup(table, col, row) function. The script modifies player.health and enemy.health to apply damage, and sets combat.last_result ('player_wounds_enemy', 'enemy_wounds_player', 'tie', 'simultaneous') and combat.last_damage. Any tables from combat_system.details are available as globals (e.g., combat_results_table).

post_round_script.description: Optional Lua script executed when the player chooses the post-round action (e.g., an optional post-round Luck test, a wound-shock roll, or a regeneration check). Same sandbox as round_script, plus player_stats and initial_stats globals containing all player stat values. Can set player.stats_changed = {stat_name = new_value} to modify stats. Set to null (or omit) when the book has no post-round phase.

post_round_label.description: Button label for the optional post-round action (e.g., 'Test Your Luck?', 'Rally', 'Regenerate?'). Only shown if post_round_script is defined. Set to null when there is no post-round script.

player_attack_strength.description: Informational: formula for player attack. e.g., '2d6 + current_skill'.

player_wounded_enemy_lucky.description: Damage dealt when player wounds enemy and is lucky.

player_wounded_enemy_unlucky.description: Damage dealt when player wounds enemy and is unlucky.

enemy_wounded_player_lucky.description: Damage taken when enemy wounds player and player is lucky.

enemy_wounded_player_unlucky.description: Damage taken when enemy wounds player and player is unlucky.

standing_modifiers.description: Schema v1.8+. Book-wide combat modifiers that apply to EVERY combat in the book unless shadowed by a per-section or per-enemy modifier with the same target. Use for rules the book's rules section states as standing rules rather than per-encounter rules — the canonical example is Lone Wolf's 'If you enter combat with no weapons, deduct 4 points from your COMBAT SKILL,' which applies to every combat across the whole book, not to any specific encounter. Each entry is a `combat_modifier` object with the same shape used by per-section and per-enemy modifiers (target dot-path, signed delta, optional condition, optional reason, optional duration). The emulator merges standing_modifiers with per-section combat_modifiers on the combat event and per-enemy intrinsic_modifiers from the enemies_catalog at combat start, evaluates each entry's condition, and freezes the surviving list for the fight. Conditions gate standing_modifiers the same way they gate per-section modifiers — the canonical no-weapon rule becomes `{target: 'player.attack', delta: -4, condition: {type: 'not', condition: {type: 'has_equipped_in_slot', slot: 'weapon'}}, reason: 'No weapon in hand'}`. See Rule 23 in the codex doc for the full specification and worked examples.

when_usable.description: When provisions can be consumed. Set to null (or omit) when provisions are disabled for this book (`enabled: false`). When enabled, pick the value that matches the book's rules text: 'when_instructed' (the book prompts eat_meal events at specific sections), 'anytime_outside_combat' (the player can choose to eat at any non-combat pause), or 'any_time' (the player can eat mid-combat too).

display_name.description: Display name for the provisions resource used in the stat bar and UI strings. Defaults to 'Provisions' if omitted. Use whatever term the book's rules section uses for its edible supplies — examples across gamebooks include 'Meals', 'Provisions', 'Rations', 'Food', 'Supplies', etc. The internal resource name used in event and condition references is still `provisions` regardless of display name; this field only affects what the UI renders.

character_creation_step.description: A single step in the character creation flow. Actions run in order during the pre-play setup; each action either prompts the player for input, deterministically sets state, or does both.

action.description: The step's action. `roll_stat` prompts a die roll for a stat (writes into `state.stats[stat_name]`). `roll_resource` prompts a die roll for a canonical resource slot or declared-stat-currency — the rolled total goes into `state.gold` / `state.provisions` / `state.meals` when `resource` matches a canonical lowercase name, or into `state.stats[resource]` when `resource` matches a declared `rules.stats[].name`. Use `roll_resource` (not `roll_stat`) for starting Gold Crowns, starting Provisions, or any other resource whose value is determined by a die roll but whose target slot is the canonical resource slot rather than a free-form stat name — see Rule 11 for the full story and the LW1 Gold Crowns worked example. `choose_one` prompts a single-option pick from a list. `choose_abilities` prompts the player to pick N items from the abilities list. `add_item` deterministically adds a starting item to inventory. `set_resource` deterministically sets a named resource (provisions, gold, or any declared stat whose name matches a `rules.stats[].name`) to a fixed amount — if the name matches a declared stat, the emulator routes the value into `state.stats[name]` so books that carry currency or experience as a named stat get the right behavior. `set_ability_uses` deterministically initializes the remaining-uses counter for a per-use ability (spells, limited talents, potions-as-ability, etc.) when the book has abilities whose use count is tracked separately from a d6 count or a discrete inventory item — for example, a spell-casting ability with a fixed number of castings per adventure, or a limited-use healing power. `distribute_points` (schema v1.10+) prompts the player to distribute a fixed total of points across two or more declared stats, within per-stat min/max ranges — the canonical point-buy character creation mechanism used by series such as Chronicles of Arborell (Windhammer) where the rules say "you have 50 points to distribute among your five attributes, with each between 5 and 11". The sum of allocated values must equal `total_points` exactly; each stat's value must fall within its declared `[min, max]` range; the resulting values are written to both `state.stats[name]` and `state.initialStats[name]` so the values are treated as the player's initial maxima for `initial_is_max` clamping (matching `roll_stat` semantics). See Rule 26 for the full story. Schema v1.6+ adds `roll_resource` (before v1.6, the only encoding for roll-to-starting-resource was to use `roll_stat` with a scratch-stat name and hope the player didn't notice the scratch stat never flowed to the canonical slot — that was the LW1 Gold Crowns data bug).

stat.description: roll_stat, set_resource (when resource matches a stat name): which stat to target.

formula.description: roll_stat: the dice formula to evaluate (e.g., '2d6*4', 'R10+10').

resource.description: set_resource: resource name. Canonical lowercase names are `gold`, `provisions`, and `meals`. Book-specific stat names (uppercased like 'GOLD', 'EXPERIENCE', or whatever the book declares) are also accepted; if the name matches a `rules.stats[].name`, the value is written to `state.stats[name]` instead of to the canonical resource slot, so books that treat their currency as a first-class stat get consistent display and condition behavior.

ability.description: set_ability_uses: the ability name (must match one of the names in `rules.abilities.available[].name` or be added as a character creation selection).

uses.description: set_ability_uses: the initial number of remaining uses for this ability. The emulator stores this in `state.abilityUses[ability]` and decrements on use.

total_points.description: distribute_points: the total number of points the player must distribute across the stats in `stats`. The sum of allocated values must equal this total exactly. Schema v1.10+.

name.description: Stat name — must match a name in `rules.stats[]`.

min.description: Inclusive minimum value the player may allocate to this stat.

max.description: Inclusive maximum value the player may allocate to this stat.

stats.description: distribute_points: the declared stats the player must distribute points among, each with a `[min, max]` inclusive range. Every `name` must appear in `rules.stats[]`. The emulator validates that the sum of allocated values equals `total_points` and that each allocated value lies within its declared range; on confirm, the values are written to both `state.stats[name]` and `state.initialStats[name]`. Schema v1.10+.

condition.description: Optional condition that must evaluate true for this step to execute. Null or absent = always runs (the common case). When present, the emulator evaluates the condition against the state *as it exists at the moment this step is reached in the creation flow* — so later steps can be gated on earlier steps' outputs (ability picks made in step 2, rolled stat values from step 1, etc.). Schema v1.6+. Introduced to support Rule 15-style discipline-gated character-creation rolls, e.g. LW1's Weaponskill weapon-type roll that should only fire if the player picked the Weaponskill discipline in the preceding choose_abilities step. When the condition evaluates false, the step is skipped entirely (no roll prompt, no state mutation, no UI display) and the flow advances to the next step.

source.description: Parser note: where in the source this was confirmed.

text.description: Full narrative text of the section.

first_10_words.description: First 10 words exactly as they appear in the source, for verification.

events.description: Events that occur in this section, processed in order.

choices.description: Navigation choices available to the player.

is_ending.description: True if this section has no outgoing navigation (death, victory, or other terminal state).

needs_review.description: If true, this section was flagged for human review during parsing.

is_subroutine_entry.description: If true, this section is the entry point of a subroutine — a self-contained mechanic (wandering monster table, silver weapon combat, re-roll loop, etc.) that the player returns from afterwards. Emulators that implement auto-return should capture the caller (the section the player came from) when navigating to this section, and use it as the destination for any `return_to_caller` event encountered later in the subroutine's execution. Emulators that implement manual return (the 'purist' style, asking the player to type the reference they noted) may ignore this field entirely. Default false. See codex section 7.6.8 for the full pattern.

text.description: Choice text as it appears in the book.

target.description: Section id to navigate to. Usually an integer section number; may be a string id when navigating to a synthetic sub-section introduced by a multi-section pattern (see codex 7.6.8).

condition.description: Condition that must be met for this choice to be available. Null = always available.

A condition that evaluates to true or false based on the player's current state. Used by choices, events (for conditional event gating — see Rule 15), combat_modifiers, and damage_interactions. The emulator evaluates conditions at the moment they matter: choice conditions at section render time, event conditions at event dispatch time, combat_modifier and damage_interaction conditions once at combat start (frozen for the fight's duration). All condition types are series-neutral — the specific item, flag, stat, ability, or slot names come from the book's data, never from hardcoded enum values in the schema or emulator.

item.description: For has_item: item_id to check in inventory. For has_equipped_item: item_id to check in any equipped slot. For has_equipped_in_slot: optional item_id to require in the named slot (if omitted, the condition evaluates true as long as the slot is non-empty).

flag.description: For has_flag: flag name to check.

slot.description: For has_equipped_in_slot: the slot name to check (e.g. 'head', 'weapon', 'body', 'main_hand'). Schema v1.5+.

property.description: For has_equipped_with_property: the property tag string to look for in any currently equipped item's `properties` array. Example: `property: "silver"` returns true if the player has any equipped item whose items_catalog entry includes "silver" in its `properties` field. Typical use: Helghast-style 'only silvered weapons harm me' immunity gating on a damage_interaction. Schema v1.5+.

event.description: An event that occurs in a section. Events are processed in order before the player makes choices. Events may carry an optional `condition` field that gates execution; events whose condition evaluates to false are skipped entirely (no state mutation, no UI display).

condition.description: Optional gate that must evaluate to true for this event to fire. If the condition is present and evaluates to false at the moment the event is processed, the event is skipped entirely — no state changes, no pause, no UI — and the next event in the queue runs. Use this for discipline-, class-, item-, or flag-driven exemptions from rule-mandated mechanics, wherever the book's rules section describes them. The general shape is `{type: 'not', condition: {type: 'has_ability', ability: '<name>'}}` (or `has_item`, `has_flag`, `stat_gte`, etc.). Examples: a discipline or class exempting the player from a per-event requirement (for instance, a Ranger class being exempt from food/meal requirements → conditional `eat_meal`); a conditional narrative stat penalty ('if you have the lantern, continue safely; otherwise lose 2 STAMINA' → conditional `modify_stat` gated on `not has_item lantern`); a conditional pickup ('if your backpack has room, take the extra meal' → conditional `add_item`); a flag-gated event ('if you have already visited the shrine, gain LUCK' → conditional `modify_stat` gated on `has_flag visited_shrine`). The schema and emulators are series-neutral — the specific ability, item, or flag names live in the data, not in the mechanism. Null or absent means the event always fires. See codex doc Rule 15 for the full encoding rule and required usage.

optional.description: modify_stat, add_item: player can choose whether to take/apply this.

modify_initial.description: modify_stat: when true, also apply `amount` to `initialStats[stat]` so the adjustment is permanent (reduces or raises the stat's ceiling). Use for book mechanics that say 'your INITIAL stat is reduced by N' or 'increase your INITIAL stat by N'. When false (or absent) the modify_stat only adjusts the current value, which healing can restore up to the unchanged initial.

category.description: remove_inventory_category (schema v1.8+): the `inventory_category` name to purge from the player's inventory. Every item in `state.inventory` whose `items_catalog[id].inventory_category` matches this string is removed; any of those items currently occupying an equipment slot is also unequipped. Use for book events that say things like 'the Kraan has ripped away your Backpack. You have lost the Pack and all the Equipment that was inside it' (LW1 §188 canonical example) — a single-event way to remove every backpack-category item at once, rather than emitting one `remove_item` per id. The category name is the string the book's items_catalog uses in its own `inventory_category` field; common values in existing books are `backpack`, `special`, `weapons`, `armor`, but the schema does not enumerate them — whatever the book declares is legal. See Rule 24 in the codex doc for the full specification.

enemies.description: combat (multiple enemies): array of enemy references.

mode.description: combat: how multiple enemies are fought.

win_to.description: combat: section id to go to on victory. Integer or string (see codex 7.6.8 for string sub-section ids).

flee_to.description: combat: section id to go to when fleeing. Null = cannot flee.

special_rules.description: combat: per-encounter special rules in plain English. Purely informational — the emulator displays this text above the combat panel but does not interpret it. For mechanically-enforced modifiers, use the structured `combat_modifiers` field alongside this one; the two can coexist (the string documents the rule in the book's narrative language, the structured field encodes the math).

combat_modifiers.description: combat: an ordered list of generic per-combat modifiers that the emulator applies to playerData / enemyData before each round_script invocation. Each modifier has a target dot-path (e.g. 'player.attack', 'player.hit_threshold', 'enemy.armor', 'enemy.attack', 'player.damage_bonus'), a signed numeric delta, and optional condition + reason fields. Modifiers whose condition evaluates to false at combat start are skipped; passing modifiers stay in effect for the duration of the fight. The emulator adds the delta to the current value of the target field on the relevant side (player or enemy) before calling the round_script, so scripts that read `player.attack` or `enemy.armor` as-is automatically see the modified value. Books that want to read the raw value can access `combat.modifiers` inside the round_script — it's exposed as an array of `{target, delta, reason}` objects. Modifiers are a series-agnostic mechanism: the target dot-paths reference whatever numeric fields the book's round_script cares about. For threshold-based combat systems (attack_stat: null), use targets like `player.hit_threshold` or `player.weapon_bonus` or `enemy.armor`. For attack-vs-attack systems, use `player.attack` and `enemy.attack`. For per-enemy intrinsic traits (rules that travel with the enemy across every section it appears in), use the `intrinsic_modifiers` field on the enemy's catalog entry instead of repeating the modifier on every combat event.

damage_interactions.description: combat: Schema v1.5+. An ordered list of per-encounter damage_interactions — immunities, resistances, and weaknesses that scale the damage flowing through the combat. Each entry has a `kind` (immunity/resistance/weakness), an optional `multiplier` override, an optional `direction` (incoming/outgoing), optional source filters (`source_has_any`/`source_lacks_all`), an optional `condition` gating the interaction on player state, and an optional human-readable `reason`. The emulator evaluates each entry's `condition` at combat start (frozen for the fight), then applies the passing entries to each damage component reported by the round_script, multiplying damage by the appropriate factor (0 for immunity, 0.5 for resistance, 2.0 for weakness, or whatever `multiplier` overrides). Use per-encounter `damage_interactions` for situational rules that apply only to this specific fight (e.g., 'in this chamber, fire damage is doubled'); for traits that belong to the enemy type itself, use the enemy's `intrinsic_damage_interactions` field instead. See Rule 18 for the full specification.

interrupt_on_first_player_hit.description: combat: if true, redirect after player's first successful wound.

success_to.description: stat_test, custom: section id on success. Integer or string.

failure_to.description: stat_test, custom: section id on failure. Integer or string.

deduct_after.description: stat_test: deduct from stat after testing.

deduct_stat.description: stat_test: which stat to deduct from.

failure_penalty.description: stat_test: additional penalty on failure (e.g., stat loss).

success_bonus.description: stat_test, roll_dice: additional bonus on success.

results.description: roll_dice: mapping of result ranges to outcomes. Keys are either a single face value ('1', '6') or an inclusive range ('1-5', '10-12').

target.description: Section id to navigate to for this roll result. Integer or string. May be null to indicate 'fall through' semantics: when the range hits and target is null, the emulator does not navigate — it continues to the next event in the section, or presents the section's choices if there are no more events. Use this for roll branches that produce a side effect (via an accompanying text or a per-range effects array) but leave the player on the current section, e.g., 'roll 1d6: 1-3 the lock holds, you may try again; 4-6 the lock breaks, turn to 42'.

effects.description: Schema v1.8+. Per-range side effects applied when this range matches. Each entry is a full event object from the shared event definition (`modify_stat`, `add_item`, `remove_item`, `set_flag`, `remove_inventory_category`, `script`, etc.). Applied in order AFTER the range match is determined and BEFORE the `target` navigation (if any), so a range entry can both change state and navigate in a single roll. Use this for the canonical Pattern 7.6.9 shape where one branch of a roll attaches a mechanical side effect — e.g. 'if the number is 4 or lower, you have fallen. Lose 2 ENDURANCE points and turn to 140. If the number is 5 or higher, turn to 323' encodes as `results: { '0-4': { effects: [{ type: 'modify_stat', stat: 'ENDURANCE', amount: -2 }], target: 140 }, '5-9': { target: 323 } }`. Effects apply regardless of whether `target` is set; an effects-only range with `target: null` produces a stat change and leaves the player on the current section. See Rule 22 in the codex doc for the full specification.

apply_to_stat.description: roll_dice: instead of navigating, apply the roll result as a stat modification. Value is the stat name. Use with amount_sign to control direction.

amount_sign.description: roll_dice + apply_to_stat: whether the roll result is added ('positive') or subtracted ('negative') from the stat. Defaults to 'negative'.

post_combat.description: roll_dice: if true, this roll occurs after a combat in the same section.

prompt.description: input_number, input_text, return_to_caller: prompt text shown to the player. For return_to_caller this text is displayed by 'purist' emulators asking the player to type the reference they noted, and by 'reference' emulators as a graceful-degradation surface when the return stack is empty (e.g. the player arrived via a debug jump with no caller recorded). Always write a real, readable prompt — never a placeholder.

target.description: input_number: 'computed' means navigate to the entered number.

answers.description: input_text: mapping of accepted answers to outcomes.

case_sensitive.description: input_text: whether answer matching is case-sensitive.

default.description: input_text: outcome when no answer matches.

required.description: eat_meal: if true, eating is mandatory.

penalty_stat.description: eat_meal: stat penalized if no food available.

penalty_amount.description: eat_meal: penalty amount if no food.

script_code.description: script: Lua code to execute. Same sandbox as combat round_script (roll, log, lookup). Also has: game_state (table of all player stats), inventory (array of item IDs), flags (array of flag names). Can modify game_state, call log(), and set navigate_to=N to go to a section.

description.description: custom, script: plain-English description of what the mechanic does.

number.description: For numbered items used in computed navigation (e.g., keys). Null for unnumbered items.

inventory_category.description: Which inventory category this item belongs to (for games with category limits).

stat_modifier.description: Passive stat modifier granted by owning or equipping this item. `when: "always"` applies whenever the item is in inventory. `when: "combat"` applies only during combat rounds, regardless of equipped state. `when: "equipped"` applies only when the item occupies one of the player's equipment slots (see `equippable` and `slot` below). Schema v1.5+ emulators honor all three values; pre-v1.5 emulators treated "equipped" and "combat" as no-ops.

equippable.description: Schema v1.5+. If true, this item can occupy an equipment slot (see `slot`) and its equipped state contributes to the player's `player.equipment` map. Non-equippable items (potions, gold, keys, quest tokens, most consumables) ignore the rest of the equipment fields and are carried only in inventory. Default false when absent.

slot.description: Schema v1.5+. The slot name this item occupies when equipped. Free-form string — the schema does not enforce a vocabulary, but the following names are suggested for consistency across books: `head`, `body`, `weapon`, `main_hand`, `off_hand`, `shield`, `feet`, `hands`, `neck`, `finger_1`, `finger_2`, `back`, `waist`, `wrist_1`, `wrist_2`. Books may invent new slot names freely for genres the suggested list doesn't cover (e.g. a naval game could use `mast_lookout`, a sci-fi game could use `cybernetic_arm`). A slot holds exactly one item at a time; equipping a new item to an occupied slot unequips the previous occupant (the previous item stays in inventory, it's just no longer equipped). Required when `equippable: true`.

equip_timing.description: Schema v1.5+. Controls when the player may equip or unequip this item. `always` = any time including mid-combat (rare — for systems where weapon swapping is a tactical choice during a fight). `out_of_combat` = only when combat is not active, which is the default for most equipment. `once` = equipped at character creation or at the moment of acquisition and cannot be unequipped or swapped afterward (cursed items, permanent magical attunements, grafted implants). The emulator enforces the timing on player-driven equip/unequip actions; it does not prevent automatic equip from `add_item` with `auto_equip: true`, which happens at any time (the moment of acquisition is considered 'not yet in combat' for timing purposes, even if a combat event is currently running). Default `out_of_combat` when absent.

auto_equip.description: Schema v1.5+. If true (the default when absent), an equippable item is automatically placed into its slot when added to inventory via `add_item`, displacing any previous occupant of that slot. Matches the typical 'you pick up the helmet and put it on' narrative for most gamebook equipment. Set to false for items the player must consciously choose to equip — a cursed ring the player finds but shouldn't wear unknowingly, a second weapon the player might not want as their active, an experimental potion in armor form, etc. Only meaningful when `equippable: true`.

properties.description: Schema v1.5+. Free-form tag strings describing intrinsic properties of this item. Used by the `has_equipped_with_property` condition and by damage_interaction source-filtering (see the `damage_interaction` definition and Rule 18). The schema does not enumerate legal property names — books define their own vocabulary based on the mechanics they need to express. Common examples: `silver`, `blessed`, `magical`, `cold_iron`, `fire`, `cold`, `holy`, `poison`, `edged`, `blunt`, `piercing`, `ranged`, `two_handed`. A book's items_catalog declares which items have which properties, and the book's enemies_catalog (or per-encounter combat events) declares which interactions key on which properties. Tags are case-sensitive; use a consistent convention within a book. Default empty when absent.

consume.description: Schema v1.9+ (Rule 25). Declares this item as a named consumable whose effects fire when the player chooses to consume it. Currently the only exposed trigger is `satisfies_eat_meal`: if true, the item appears as an additional action during any `eat_meal` pause where the player holds at least one of it, alongside the standard `eat` (generic provision) and `skip` actions. Selecting the named-consumable action removes one of the item from inventory (auto-unequipping if currently equipped, per the usual remove_item semantics), dispatches every event in `effects` synchronously through the event handler, and satisfies the `eat_meal` prompt without decrementing `state.provisions` — named consumables are a substitute for generic provisions, not a supplement that also burns one. Pausing events inside `effects` are not supported: if a sub-event returns 'pause' (nested combat, eat_meal, stat_test, input_number, etc.), the remaining effects are skipped with a warning; encode pause-requiring shapes as section-level events with a player-facing choice instead. Typical shape for LW-family healing herbs is a single `modify_stat` with the book's health stat and a positive delta, honouring the usual `initial_is_max` clamp via `modify_stat`'s own logic.

satisfies_eat_meal.description: If true, the item is offered as an alternative in every `eat_meal` pause where the player holds at least one. Default false when absent (meaning the `consume` block is inert — reserved for future trigger types like unconditional `use_item` events).

effects.description: Event list dispatched synchronously when the item is consumed. Every entry is a standard event (typically `modify_stat`, but any non-pausing event type is legal: `modify_stat`, `add_item`, `remove_item`, `set_flag`, `remove_inventory_category`, `script` that does not pause). Fires AFTER the item is removed from inventory and BEFORE the eat_meal pause is cleared, so `modify_stat` clamps (like `initial_is_max`) apply correctly. Do NOT include a `remove_item` for the consumed item itself — the emulator handles the removal automatically.

enemy.description: An enemy encountered in combat. Stats are carried as top-level properties whose keys match the stat names declared in `rules.stats[].name` (and specifically whatever `rules.attack_stat` and `rules.health_stat` point at). The schema does not enumerate specific stat names because gamebook series use different ones: Fighting Fantasy enemies have SKILL / STAMINA, Lone Wolf enemies have COMBAT SKILL / ENDURANCE, Advanced Dungeons & Dragons Adventure Gamebooks have AC / HP / THAC0, other series use STRENGTH / LIFE POINTS, and so on. Use whatever names the book's rules section declares; the emulators normalize stat-name lookups so spacing and case variants all resolve.

section.description: Section number where this enemy first appears, for cross-referencing and debugging. Optional.

special.description: Free-form text describing any per-enemy special combat rules as the book's narrative presents them (e.g. 'Deduct 1 from your COMBAT SKILL for this fight due to the enemy's poisonous breath'). For mechanical enforcement of these rules the enemy should also carry a structured `intrinsic_modifiers` block — see Rule 17 in the codex doc. Leaving this as plain text is acceptable for enemies whose special rules are purely flavor, but for books where the enemy has a combat-affecting intrinsic (like 'Deduct 2 unless you have Mindshield', 'immune to fire damage', '+2 to its own attacks when wounded') the structured form is preferred because it travels with the enemy across every section it appears in instead of needing to be restated on each combat event.

intrinsic_modifiers.description: Schema v1.4+. Per-enemy combat modifiers that apply every time this enemy is encountered, across every section that references this catalog entry. Each modifier has the same shape as a `combat_modifier` (target dot-path, signed delta, optional condition, optional reason). Use intrinsic_modifiers for rules that are properties of the *enemy type* rather than of a specific encounter: Vordak / Helghast / Gourgaz in Lone Wolf all have a '-2 COMBAT SKILL unless Mindshield' rule that applies wherever they appear; undead enemies in many series have 'only killable on a roll of N+' rules that travel with the creature. The emulator merges intrinsic_modifiers from every enemy in the combat with any per-section combat_modifiers on the combat event itself, then evaluates conditions once at combat start and applies the passing deltas to playerData / enemyData before the round_script runs. Per-enemy intrinsics and per-encounter modifiers are additive — if a section has a surprise attack (+4 player attack) and the enemy has a '-2 unless Mindshield' intrinsic, the player who doesn't have Mindshield gets +4 - 2 = +2. See codex doc Rule 17.

intrinsic_damage_interactions.description: Schema v1.5+. Per-enemy damage_interactions (immunities, resistances, weaknesses) that travel with this enemy type across every section it appears in. Canonical example: Helghast in Lone Wolf 2 are immune to any weapon that is not silver, blessed, or the Sommerswerd — encoded as `{kind: 'immunity', source_lacks_all: ['silver', 'blessed', 'sommerswerd'], reason: 'Helghast are harmed only by silvered or blessed weapons'}`. Other examples: a fire elemental resisting blunt damage and taking double from cold (two separate entries), an undead creature immune to poison damage, a golem taking half damage from edged weapons. The emulator merges intrinsic_damage_interactions from every enemy in the combat with any per-section damage_interactions on the combat event itself, evaluates the optional conditions once at combat start, and applies the surviving entries to each damage component the round_script reports. Source filtering (`source_has_any` / `source_lacks_all`) happens per-component per-round because the source set depends on what the round_script emits that round. See codex doc Rule 18 for the full specification and worked examples.

additionalProperties.description: Any stat or flag the book's rules define. Numeric stats use the stat names from `rules.stats[].name`; string-valued fields like alignment, creature type, or narrative tags are also allowed. Boolean flags like `incorporeal` or `immune_to_mundane_weapons` may be used where the round_script reads them.

combat_modifier.description: A single combat modifier. Schema v1.4+. Modifiers are emitted in two places: on `combat` events (via the event's `combat_modifiers` array) for per-section narrative bonuses and penalties, and on `enemies_catalog` entries (via the entry's `intrinsic_modifiers` array) for traits that travel with the enemy type across every section it appears in. Both use the same shape. The emulator merges the two lists at combat start, evaluates each modifier's optional condition, and applies the passing deltas to playerData / enemyData before each round_script invocation. Modifiers are snapshotted at combat start — a mid-combat state change does not re-evaluate conditions. For dynamic per-round logic, encode it in the round_script directly.

target.description: Dot-path identifying which numeric field the delta is applied to. The prefix is `player` or `enemy`; the field after the dot is whatever numeric field the round_script reads. Examples: `player.attack` (attack-vs-attack systems), `player.hit_threshold` and `enemy.armor` (threshold-based systems like GrailQuest), `player.damage_bonus`, `enemy.attack`, `player.weapon_bonus`. The target is free-form because different gamebook series use different fields — the schema does not enumerate them. Fields the round_script doesn't read are harmless (the modifier is still applied to the data object, but has no effect on the round math). Fields that are currently undefined on the data object are treated as 0 so books can introduce new fields purely via modifiers.

delta.description: Signed numeric delta added to the target field. Positive values buff, negative values penalize. The modifier stays in effect for the duration of the combat.

condition.description: Optional gate evaluated once at combat start. If the condition evaluates to false the modifier is NOT applied. Uses the same condition union as event/choice conditions (`has_item`, `has_flag`, `stat_gte`, `stat_lte`, `has_ability`, `not`, `and`, `or`, `test_failed`, `test_succeeded`). Typical uses: 'Deduct 2 COMBAT SKILL unless you have Mindshield' = `{target: 'player.attack', delta: -2, condition: {type: 'not', condition: {type: 'has_ability', ability: 'Mindshield'}}}`; 'Deduct 3 COMBAT SKILL if you do not have a torch' = `{target: 'player.attack', delta: -3, condition: {type: 'not', condition: {type: 'has_item', item: 'torch'}}}`. Null or absent means the modifier always applies.

reason.description: Human-readable explanation of why the modifier applies. Shown in the combat UI's modifier box so the player knows what's in effect, and logged when the playthrough record is generated. Not interpreted by the emulator; purely for display and debugging. Examples: 'Surprise attack', 'No torch in the dark', 'Dust attack', 'Mindblast bonus', 'Wounded opponent'.

duration.description: How long the modifier applies within a combat. `fight` (default when absent): applies every round of the fight. `first_round`: applies only in round 1, then drops off. `after_first_round` (schema v1.7+): applies in round 2 and every subsequent round, but NOT in round 1 — used for section-specific rules like 'add 2 to COMBAT SKILL for the first round only (surprise), then deduct 2 from COMBAT SKILL for the second and subsequent rounds' where the penalty is explicitly scoped to rounds after the first. `round` is reserved for a future per-round dynamic semantic and is currently treated as `fight` by the emulators. Schema v1.7.0 / emulators v3.2.0 honor `first_round` and `after_first_round`; earlier versions treated all values as `fight`. Conditions are still snapshotted at combat start (a modifier gated on an ability the player expends mid-fight stays in effect until the fight ends) — duration narrows WHICH ROUNDS the frozen modifier participates in, not whether the condition is re-evaluated.

damage_interaction.description: Schema v1.5+. A single damage_interaction — an immunity, resistance, or weakness that scales damage flowing through a combat by a multiplicative factor. Where `combat_modifier` (schema v1.4) handles additive per-fight bonuses and penalties that modify inputs to the round_script (e.g., 'add 4 to COMBAT SKILL for this fight'), `damage_interaction` handles multiplicative adjustments to the *output* of the round_script (e.g., 'take zero damage from non-silver weapons', 'take double damage from fire'). The two mechanisms are independent and compose: a combat can have both modifiers (changing what the script sees as input) and interactions (scaling the damage the script produces as output). Interactions live in two places: `damage_interactions` on combat events (for per-encounter situational rules) and `intrinsic_damage_interactions` on enemies_catalog entries (for traits that travel with the enemy type). Both use this same shape. The emulator merges both lists at combat start, evaluates each entry's optional `condition`, and applies the passing entries to each damage component reported by the round_script, per round. Source filtering (via `source_has_any` / `source_lacks_all`) happens per-component per-round because the source tags on damage components depend on what the round_script reports each round. See codex doc Rule 18 for the full specification.

kind.description: Required. Semantic category driving the default multiplier: `immunity` = 0 (no damage applied), `resistance` = 0.5 (half damage), `weakness` = 2.0 (double damage). The kind is also surfaced in the combat UI so the player sees a label like 'Immune' / 'Resistant' / 'Weak' alongside the numeric effect, rather than a bare multiplier. Choose the kind that matches the narrative intent; override the default with `multiplier` if the book specifies a non-standard value.

multiplier.description: Optional override for the damage multiplier. If omitted, the default for the `kind` is used (immunity=0, resistance=0.5, weakness=2.0). Use an explicit value for non-standard cases: 0.25 for 'heavy resistance', 1.5 for 'mild weakness', 3.0 for 'triple damage from fire on the fire-vulnerable troll'. Must be ≥ 0. Negative multipliers are not supported — an interaction cannot turn damage into healing; if a book needs that, use a modify_stat event or a round_script health adjustment instead. Values greater than 1 should use `kind: 'weakness'`; values less than 1 but greater than 0 should use `kind: 'resistance'`; 0 should use `kind: 'immunity'`. The emulator does not enforce this convention but the UI labels are chosen from `kind`, so mismatched values will display confusingly.

direction.description: Which damage flow the interaction applies to. `incoming` (default when absent) means damage dealt TO the enemy BY the player — the common case, used for 'enemy is immune to non-silver weapons' and similar rules. `outgoing` means damage dealt BY the enemy TO the player — used for 'player is resistant to fire damage while wearing the dragon amulet' or 'player takes double damage from this specific boss's breath weapon'. The emulator scales the relevant damage value (damage_to_enemy or damage_to_player) by the interaction's multiplier before subtracting from health.

source_has_any.description: Optional source-tag filter. If present and non-empty, the interaction applies only to damage components whose source list contains at least one of the listed tags. Examples: `['fire']` matches fire-tagged damage only; `['silver', 'blessed']` matches damage tagged silver OR blessed. Use this for 'double damage from fire' (weakness with source_has_any: ['fire']) or 'half damage from blunt or piercing' (resistance with source_has_any: ['blunt', 'piercing']). Omit (or use an empty array) for an interaction that should apply regardless of source tags. Source filtering is evaluated per-component per-round because a single attack can produce multiple damage components with different tags; this is what makes compound-damage cases like a poisoned silver weapon against a poison-immune silver-weak enemy work correctly. See Rule 18 for worked examples.

source_lacks_all.description: Optional inverse source-tag filter. If present and non-empty, the interaction applies only to damage components whose source list contains NONE of the listed tags. Canonical use: Helghast immunity — `{kind: 'immunity', source_lacks_all: ['silver', 'blessed', 'sommerswerd']}` → any damage component whose sources do not contain silver, blessed, or sommerswerd is zeroed. Damage tagged with `['silver']` bypasses the immunity because the component's sources include 'silver'; untagged damage (source list empty) is still matched because it contains none of the listed tags. The two source filters (`source_has_any` and `source_lacks_all`) can be combined on a single interaction — both must pass for the interaction to apply. See Rule 18 for worked examples.

condition.description: Optional gate evaluated once at combat start. Same condition union as combat_modifiers and event/choice conditions (`has_item`, `has_flag`, `stat_gte`, `stat_lte`, `has_ability`, `has_equipped_item`, `has_equipped_in_slot`, `has_equipped_with_property`, `not`, `and`, `or`, `test_failed`). Condition filtering gates the entire interaction on player/world state; source filtering (`source_has_any` / `source_lacks_all`) gates per damage component on what the round_script emits. The two filters compose: a condition of `{has_equipped_with_property: 'holy'}` combined with `source_has_any: ['undead_slayer']` means 'this interaction applies only when the player has a holy-tagged equipped item AND the damage component is tagged with undead_slayer'. Condition filtering is frozen at combat start so mid-combat state changes do not re-evaluate. Null or absent means the interaction is not condition-gated.

reason.description: Human-readable explanation of why the interaction applies. Shown in the combat UI's damage interactions panel so the player knows what's in effect, and written to the playthrough log when the interaction fires. Not interpreted by the emulator; purely for display and debugging. Examples: 'Helghast are harmed only by silvered or blessed weapons', 'Fire elemental is disrupted by cold', 'Skeleton is immune to poison', 'Undead take double damage from holy attacks'.
